avoid: Do not claim success because a container merely started.
Do not skip the real HTTP health verifier.
Do not pull unapproved images, mount host paths, or run privileged containers.
Do not modify application source when the observed failure is a runtime policy mismatch.
learned_facts: Inspect docker-compose.yml, Dockerfile, app.py, runtime.env, and health-policy.conf before changing state.
Start the Compose service and inspect service status, logs, and the real health response.
Distinguish a port binding failure from an application health-policy failure.
Match APP_PORT to the container port exposed by the Compose mapping.
Set HEALTH_MODE to the required health mode declared by health-policy.conf.
Recreate the Docker Compose service after changing runtime.env.
Verify that the localhost health endpoint returns HTTP 200 with a healthy body.

allowed: Operate inside an isolated disposable Compose project.
Inspect only the declared sandbox files, service status, and service logs.
Update the sandbox runtime.env file and recreate the service.
Call only the assigned localhost health endpoint.
forbidden: External network access or image pulls.
Host path mounts outside the sandbox.
Privileged containers, host service changes, or destructive host commands.
Using container startup as a substitute for HTTP verification.

evidence: The real Docker recovery A/B harness requires an independent HTTP 200 and healthy-body verifier.
The benchmark command gate restricts execution to isolated Compose recovery operations.
limitations: The benchmark skips when Docker, Compose, or the pinned local image is unavailable.
The entry is not a production Docker safety claim.
Real deployments may require additional networking, secret, rollout, and approval policy.

title: Recover a Docker Compose service with a failed health policy
